conflict: The internal pressure of weeks of failed practice reaching a tipping point, and the external impossibility of sharing the achievement with anyone who can verify it
consequence: Gavin performs his first successful First Art edit: adjusting the substrate rules governing visibility around a quill. The distinction between this and a Disillusionment Charm is the novel's central epistemological problem — the difference is invisible to anyone who cannot perceive the substrate. Physical cost: nosebleed, blurred vision
must_land_beats: The quill experiment: the distinction between making something invisible (a spell applied to an object) and loosening the agreement that makes visibility apply to the object. The quill is not hidden — it is less subject to being seen. The chapter must render this precisely
The physical cost: nosebleed, visual distortion for an hour. The First Art taxes cognitive architecture, not magical energy
Showing Miren: she sees a blurry quill and interprets it as a bad Disillusionment Charm. She is not cruel about it. She is telling the truth as she can see it
The specific loneliness of knowing something no one else can verify — not dramatic isolation but the dull, practical loneliness of being unable to share your work
pressure_source: The internal pressure of weeks of failed practice reaching a tipping point, and the external impossibility of sharing the achievement with anyone who can verify it
